info.topictitle: B3.2 Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes (HL only)

markdown: # B3.2 Fundamentals of OOP for Multiple Classes (HL) ## Inheritance ### Understanding Inheritance Inheritance is one of the core pillars of Object-Oriented Programming, allowing us to create new classes based on existing ones. Think of it as creating a family tree for your classes! ### How Inheritance Works When a class (child/subclass) inherits from another class (parent/superclass), it gets access to: * All public and protected methods * All public and protected attributes * The ability to override existing methods * The ability to add new methods and attributes ## Polymorphism ### Dynamic Polymorphism This is the ability of objects to take on multiple forms. The most common type is method overriding, where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that's already defined in its superclass. ## Abstract Classes Abstract classes serve as blueprints for other classes. They can't be instantiated directly but can define both concrete and abstract methods. ## Composition & Aggregation ### Understanding Relationships * **Composition**: A "has-a" relationship where the child cannot exist without the parent * **Aggregation**: A looser "has-a" relationship where components can exist independently ## Design Patterns ### Singleton Pattern Ensures a class has only one instance and provides global access to it. ### Factory Pattern Creates objects without exposing the instantiation logic. This covers the fundamental concepts of OOP for multiple classes at the HL level. Practice implementing these concepts in your own code to better understand how they work together in real applications.
